focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The waste-log duplicate with the boy's name on it stuck damp to Marisol's thigh as she came down the service stairs at Caldera Junction to a platform that already looked stripped for evidence.

A folding compliance table had been opened under the tin awning where crew usually smoked after shift. Two portable printers sat on it beside sealed evidence envelopes, a biometric reader, and a sweating cooler of bottled water that nobody had been invited to touch. Teresa Ordonez stood behind the table in an immaculate white shell uniform that had gone dark under the arms anyway. Her makeup held. Her cuticles did not. She kept worrying one bitten thumb with the edge of a thumbnail while a junior audit clerk beside her peeled at the blister on her heel through the slit in a brand-new shoe.

The train had rolled in forty minutes earlier with hot axles, a night of outage stink still trapped in the connector joints, and blackwater sloshing low in the tanks. Marisol had not slept. She still had mosquito bites up both forearms from Marsh Crescent. The dried salt on her collar rubbed like sandpaper each time she turned her head. She saw the compliance table, Teresa, the security pair in dark vests, and knew the deep clean had finished becoming a story.

Crew were lined up along the awning posts with their passcards already out. No one was pretending this was routine. Ivo stood halfway down the row, heavy shoulders bent, grease under his nails still black after a scrub. He had a paper cup of machine coffee in one hand and the folded court letter about his child-support review peeking from his shirt pocket because he read bad news more than once when it did not change. Paloma had come over from the trauma room in a fresh gown already damp at the spine. Her silvering braid wa...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...on purpose and well enough to make it stick.

One of the cleaners staggered past carrying another bagged mattress skin toward incineration, Tyvek pasted to his back with sweat. The day-care fee on his cracked phone would be climbing. The custody sergeant was already on a second peppermint, jaw working like a man grinding money between his teeth. The woman with the manifest fan had stopped fanning and was simply staring at the concrete. The old man with the bag between his knees did not look up at anyone.

Marisol finished the inventory sweep because that was what the role required and because stopping halfway made suspicion. She marked destroyed what had not yet been destroyed, present what she prayed might turn present later, unknown where the truth could not be reached in time. By the time she handed the board back, the car behind her was gutted to metal and the hold had swollen beyond anything she could call clean.

At the platform edge, she looked up at the departure board again. Ivo's number sat under the mechanical tag and hers under the sanitation order, red beside red. The system had room now for both lies and none for mercy.

She folded the wrapper strip smaller and slid it into the pocket with Gabriel's recorder. The two objects clicked softly against each other when she walked. Around her the train kept extracting payment for the hour she had stolen: from the workers in sweat and late fees, from the detainees in heat, wet clothes, and vanished property, from Ivo maybe in August when men in clean offices would decide whether his service record had remained acceptable enough to let him grow old less poor. When dispatch finally let the steel move again, Marisol rode south in the service alcove, and that debt rode with her in her pocket beside Gabriel's recorder.
